sourceMovie.title: Bean
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Bean works as a family movie with a point of view—fans quote moments, but they also argue about what the film is actually saying about power, desire, or survival. The family and adventure blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: One Hundred and One Dalmatians
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Bean clicked for you, One Hundred and One Dalmatians (1961) is a strong next stop: both trade in family and adventure, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Bean clicked for you, Flushed Away (2006) is a strong next stop: both trade in family and adventure, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Bean?
faq.answer: Try One Hundred and One Dalmatians, Flushed Away, The Lego Movie 2: The Second Part—each shares DNA with Bean while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Bean?
faq.answer: Bean (1997) is best described as Family, Adventure, Comedy. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Bean part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Mr. Bean Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
